exploration_log.aha_moment: Recognizing that `error_message` is always a `const char*` to a literal, so dynamic content needed a separate std::string owner to back the pointer's lifetime.
exploration_log.hindsight: Could have scanned the class definition first to understand the member type before looking at the assignment sites.

alternatives: Use a fixed char array member
key_decisions.decision: Add std::string error_message_storage member instead of changing error_message type to std::string
key_decisions.reasoning: Minimally invasive: get_error_message() returns const char* which must remain valid; adding a storage string keeps all existing const char* literal assignments unchanged

queries.query_text: add hex byte value to ill-formed UTF-8 error message in scan_string next_byte_in_range lexer.hpp

solve_notes: The `error_message` member is a `const char*` pointing to string literals, so dynamic content required an auxiliary `std::string error_message_storage` member. I patched both `next_byte_in_range()` and the `default` case in `scan_string()` to use `snprintf` (already imported via `<cstdio>`) to format the offending byte's hex value into a buffer, store it in `error_message_storage`, and point `error_message` at its `c_str()`.
task_text:
Fix lexer not including offending byte value in ill-formed UTF-8 error messages

The `scan_string()` function in `detail/input/lexer.hpp` validates
raw (non-escaped) multi-byte UTF-8 sequences and rejects ill-formed
byte sequences via `next_byte_in_range()` and the `default` case of
the inner switch. However, when an ill-formed byte is detected, the
error message is always the generic
`"invalid string: ill-formed UTF-8 byte"` without identifying the
specific offending byte value. This contrasts with the detailed
control-character error messages elsewhere in `scan_string()` (e.g.,
`"invalid string: control character U+0000 (NUL) must be escaped"`).
Fix `next_byte_in_range()` and the `default` ill-formed-byte case in
`scan_string()` to include the hex value of the offending byte in the
error message — e.g., `"invalid string: ill-formed UTF-8 byte 0xC0"`
— so that users can identify malformed bytes without additional
tooling.

test_selection.coverage_skip_reason: Auditor could not generate baseline coverage — see auditor report

thrash_preventing_defs.name: error_message
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: its type (const char*) determines why a storage std::string is needed; an agent that didn't check the type might try assigning a std::string directly

thrash_preventing_defs.name: get_error_message
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: returns const char* — confirms the interface contract that error_message must remain a pointer

thrash_preventing_defs.name: get_token_string
thrash_preventing_defs.reason: read: shows the existing snprintf + std::array pattern used in the same class for formatting — the exact idiom to reuse

tier_difference_reasoning: The minimum set covers what was actually edited. The thrash_preventing set adds `error_message` (to reveal it is a `const char*`, not `std::string`, which drives the design choice of adding a storage member), `get_error_message` (confirms the pointer return contract), and `get_token_string` (shows the existing snprintf idiom so the agent doesn't invent a different approach). Without these, an agent would likely search for the type of error_message and the existing formatting pattern before knowing how to write the fix.
